mini_quizzes.title: MQ-1 · Core ML Concepts and Terminology

questions.stem: Which best defines a 'feature' in machine learning?

options.A: The target variable a model is trained to predict
options.B: An individual measurable input variable used as a predictor in a model
options.C: A row of labelled training data
options.D: The model's learned weight vector

questions.explanation: A feature (predictor, input variable) is a measurable property used as input to the model. Features form the columns of the feature matrix X. The target variable y is separate.
questions.source: James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.1. https://www.statlearning.com

questions.stem: What is the train/validation/test split used for in supervised learning?

options.A: Train: fit the model; Validation: tune hyperparameters and select model; Test: unbiased final evaluation on held-out data
options.B: Train: clean data; Validation: visualise data; Test: train the model
options.C: All three splits are used to train the model simultaneously
options.D: Train: fit model; Test: tune hyperparameters; Validation: deploy model

questions.explanation: Train set: model parameters are fitted. Validation set: hyperparameters are tuned and models are compared — never used for final reporting. Test set: touched only once for unbiased evaluation. Using the test set during development causes data leakage and over-optimistic estimates.
questions.source: Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. O'Reilly, 2022. Ch.2. https://github.com/ageron/handson-ml3

questions.stem: Why does overfitting occur, and what are two standard techniques to detect it?

options.A: Overfitting occurs when the model is too simple; detected by high training error
options.B: Overfitting occurs when a model memorises training noise rather than learning generalisable patterns, causing high training accuracy but poor validation/test accuracy; detected by (1) comparing train vs validation loss curves and (2) k-fold cross-validation
options.C: Overfitting is caused by insufficient data volume only; detected by confusion matrix
options.D: Overfitting only occurs in neural networks, not classical models

questions.explanation: Overfitting: model capacity too high relative to data → fits noise. Detection: (1) train loss decreasing while validation loss increases (diverging curves); (2) k-fold CV showing high variance across folds. Remedies: L1/L2 regularisation, dropout, early stopping, more data.
questions.source: Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022; Hastie et al. ESL 2nd ed. 2009. https://hastie.su.domains/ElemStatLearn

questions.stem: How does the bias-variance trade-off relate to model complexity?

options.A: Both bias and variance decrease as model complexity increases
options.B: High bias (underfitting) comes from low complexity; high variance (overfitting) from high complexity; optimal complexity minimises total error = bias² + variance + irreducible noise
options.C: Bias and variance are independent of model complexity
options.D: High complexity always produces lower total error

questions.explanation: Bias²: error from wrong assumptions (underfitting); reduces as complexity increases. Variance: sensitivity to training fluctuations (overfitting); increases with complexity. Total error = bias² + variance + irreducible noise. Optimal complexity minimises their sum.
questions.source: Hastie et al. ESL 2nd ed. Ch.7. 2009; James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.2.

questions.stem: A model achieves 99% training accuracy and 61% test accuracy on a 10,000-sample dataset. Diagnose the problem and select the most appropriate remedy.

options.A: Underfitting — increase model complexity
options.B: Overfitting — 38pp train/test gap indicates high variance. Remedies: add L2 regularisation, apply dropout (if neural network), increase training data, or reduce model complexity
options.C: Data leakage — the test set was used during training
options.D: Class imbalance — use SMOTE to balance classes

questions.explanation: 99% train vs 61% test = 38pp gap — classic overfitting. The model memorised training data. First-line remedies: L2 regularisation, reduce model depth/width, dropout for neural nets, k-fold CV to verify. Data leakage would show near-identical train/test scores, not this gap.
questions.source: Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022; Hastie et al. ESL 2nd ed. 2009.

questions.stem: What does the coefficient of determination (R²) measure in linear regression?

options.A: The slope of the regression line
options.B: The proportion of variance in y explained by the model (0 = no fit, 1 = perfect fit)
options.C: The mean absolute error of predictions
options.D: The number of features in the model

questions.explanation: R² = 1 − SS_res/SS_tot. R²=1: perfect predictions. R²=0: model no better than predicting the mean. R² can be negative if the model is worse than the mean baseline.
questions.source: James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.3. https://www.statlearning.com

questions.stem: Ridge regression adds which penalty term to the OLS loss function?

options.A: L1: λ·Σ|βⱼ|
options.B: L2: λ·Σβⱼ²
options.C: Elastic net: λ₁·Σ|βⱼ| + λ₂·Σβⱼ²
options.D: No penalty — Ridge is identical to OLS

questions.explanation: Ridge (L2 regularisation): Loss = MSE + λ·Σβⱼ². The L2 penalty shrinks coefficients toward zero but rarely to exactly zero. Lasso (L1) uses λ·Σ|βⱼ| and can produce exact zeros (feature selection). Elastic net combines both.
questions.source: James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.6. https://www.statlearning.com

questions.stem: Why does Lasso regression perform implicit feature selection while Ridge does not?

options.A: Lasso uses a differentiable penalty that pushes all coefficients to exactly zero simultaneously
options.B: The L1 penalty's non-differentiability at zero creates a corner solution that sets small coefficients to exactly zero; Ridge's smooth L2 penalty shrinks but never zeroes coefficients
options.C: Lasso selects features randomly; Ridge does not
options.D: Ridge performs feature selection for categorical features only

questions.explanation: The L1 penalty |β| has a kink at β=0 (non-differentiable). Under optimisation, coefficients below a threshold are pushed to exactly zero — sparse solutions. Ridge's smooth β² has a gradient at zero that only approaches but never reaches zero.
questions.source: Hastie et al. ESL 2nd ed. Ch.3; James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.6.

questions.stem: How do MSE and MAE differ as regression loss functions, and when should each be preferred?

options.A: MSE and MAE are identical — use either interchangeably
options.B: MSE = mean of squared errors, penalises outliers heavily; MAE = mean of absolute errors, more robust to outliers. Prefer MSE when large errors are disproportionately costly; prefer MAE when outliers exist and should not dominate the loss
options.C: MSE is for classification; MAE is for regression
options.D: MAE is always lower than MSE

questions.explanation: MSE: Σ(yᵢ−ŷᵢ)²/n. Squaring penalises large residuals quadratically — sensitive to outliers but mathematically convenient (differentiable). MAE: Σ|yᵢ−ŷᵢ|/n. Linear penalty — robust to outliers but non-differentiable at zero. Use MSE for low-outlier problems; MAE for robust regression.
questions.source: James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.3; Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022.

questions.stem: A linear regression model predicting house prices has R²=0.42 on the test set. Diagnose and suggest two improvements.

options.A: R²=0.42 is excellent — no improvement needed
options.B: R²=0.42 means the model explains only 42% of price variance — likely underfitting. Improvements: (1) engineer new features (e.g., price per sqft, neighbourhood interaction terms); (2) try a more flexible model (Random Forest or Gradient Boosting)
options.C: Remove all features — fewer features always improve R²
options.D: Switch to classification — regression is inappropriate for price prediction

questions.explanation: R²=0.42 means 58% of variance is unexplained — underfitting or missing features. Steps: (1) Feature engineering: square footage × neighbourhood, property age, transit distance. (2) Non-linear models: Random Forest or XGBoost typically achieve R²>0.85 on structured tabular data.
questions.source: Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022; James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.3.

questions.stem: In a binary classification confusion matrix, what does a False Negative (FN) represent?

options.A: The model predicted positive and was correct
options.B: The model predicted negative but the true label was positive
options.C: The model predicted positive but the true label was negative
options.D: The model predicted negative and was correct

questions.explanation: Confusion matrix: TP = predicted positive, actually positive. TN = predicted negative, actually negative. FP = predicted positive, actually negative (Type I). FN = predicted negative, actually positive (Type II). In medical screening, FN = missed disease case — often the most costly error.
questions.source: James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.4; Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022.

questions.stem: Which metric is most appropriate when the cost of False Negatives is much higher than False Positives (e.g., cancer screening)?

options.A: Accuracy
options.B: Precision
options.C: Recall (Sensitivity)
options.D: Specificity

questions.explanation: Recall = TP / (TP + FN). High recall minimises FN — crucial when missing a positive case is catastrophic (cancer, fraud, defect screening). Precision = TP / (TP + FP) minimises FP. Accuracy is misleading with class imbalance.
questions.source: James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.4; Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022.

questions.stem: Why is accuracy an unreliable metric for evaluating classifiers on imbalanced datasets?

options.A: Accuracy is always the most reliable metric regardless of class distribution
options.B: A naïve classifier predicting the majority class for every example achieves high accuracy (e.g., 99% on 99:1 imbalanced data) while having zero utility — recall and F1 expose this failure
options.C: Accuracy is unreliable only for regression problems
options.D: Accuracy penalises large prediction errors more heavily than small ones

questions.explanation: On 99:1 class imbalance: predicting all-negative gives 99% accuracy but 0% recall and 0% F1. Accuracy rewards majority-class bias. Use: AUC-ROC, F1 = 2·(P·R)/(P+R), or precision-recall AUC for severe imbalance.
questions.source: Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022; James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.4.

questions.stem: How does logistic regression produce a probability output, and how does it differ from a decision tree?

options.A: Logistic regression is a decision tree variant
options.B: Logistic regression applies the sigmoid function to a linear combination of features to output P(y=1|X) ∈ (0,1); a decision tree partitions the feature space via recursive binary splits, producing non-linear axis-aligned boundaries
options.C: Both produce identical decision boundaries for all datasets
options.D: Decision trees output probabilities; logistic regression outputs class labels only

questions.explanation: Logistic: P(y=1|X) = σ(β₀+β₁X₁+…) where σ(z)=1/(1+e⁻ᶻ). Decision boundary: linear hyperplane. Decision trees: recursive splits — non-linear, axis-aligned boundaries. Trees handle non-linearity and interactions naturally; logistic regression requires explicit feature engineering for non-linear problems.
questions.source: James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.4 & Ch.8.

questions.stem: A fraud detection model has Precision=0.95, Recall=0.30, F1=0.46. The product team wants to maximise fraud capture. What does this indicate and what adjustment should be made?

options.A: The model is excellent — Precision=0.95 means 95% of flagged cases are real fraud
options.B: High precision / low recall: the model is very conservative — misses 70% of fraud cases. Adjust: lower the classification threshold (e.g., from 0.5 to 0.2) to increase recall at the cost of some precision
options.C: The model is underfitting — increase model complexity
options.D: F1=0.46 is optimal — no adjustment possible without retraining

questions.explanation: Recall=0.30 means 70% of fraudulent transactions are missed — unacceptable. The fix: lower the decision threshold (predict fraud when P>0.2 instead of 0.5). This increases recall but generates more false positives. Plot the precision-recall curve to find the optimal threshold for the business's FN/FP cost ratio.
questions.source: Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022; James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.4.

questions.stem: What does k-fold cross-validation involve?

options.A: Training k separate models on k different datasets
options.B: Splitting data into k equal folds, training on k−1 folds and validating on the remaining fold, rotating k times, then averaging the k validation scores
options.C: Applying k different algorithms to the same dataset and voting
options.D: Splitting data once into k% train and (100−k)% test

questions.explanation: k-fold CV: shuffle data, divide into k equal folds. Iterate k times: train on k−1 folds, validate on the held-out fold. Average the k validation scores for an unbiased performance estimate. Standard: k=5 or k=10.
questions.source: Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022; James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.5.

questions.stem: What is a hyperparameter in machine learning, and which of the following is an example?

options.A: A parameter learned from training data; e.g., linear regression coefficient β
options.B: A configuration set before training that controls the learning process; e.g., learning rate α, tree depth, or regularisation strength λ
options.C: A feature derived from raw data during preprocessing
options.D: The final model output after training

questions.explanation: Hyperparameters are set before training and control the learning algorithm — not learned from data. Examples: learning rate (gradient descent step size), max_depth (decision trees), C/λ (SVM/Ridge regularisation), n_estimators (Random Forest). Model parameters (β, weights) are learned from data.
questions.source: Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022.

questions.stem: What is data leakage and why does it produce misleading model evaluation results?

options.A: Data leakage occurs when training data is too large
options.B: Data leakage occurs when information from the test or future data contaminates the training process, causing the model to appear more accurate than it will be on truly unseen data
options.C: Data leakage refers to GDPR violations involving training data
options.D: Data leakage only affects deep learning models

questions.explanation: Examples: (1) scaling the full dataset before splitting (test statistics leak into train); (2) including future information as a feature. Both produce inflated validation metrics. In production, the model encounters data without these signals, causing performance collapse. Fix: fit all preprocessing only on train, apply to val/test.
questions.source: Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022; Kaufman et al. Leakage in Data Mining. TKDD, 2012.

questions.stem: When should you use AUC-ROC vs AUC-PR (Precision-Recall AUC) as the primary evaluation metric?

options.A: AUC-ROC is always superior — use it for all classification tasks
options.B: AUC-ROC summarises performance across all thresholds and suits balanced classes; AUC-PR is more informative for severe class imbalance (rare positives) because it focuses on positive class performance
options.C: AUC-PR is only for multi-class problems
options.D: Both metrics always produce the same ranking of models

questions.explanation: AUC-ROC: plots TPR vs FPR across thresholds. On highly imbalanced data (e.g., 0.1% fraud), high AUC-ROC is achievable with poor positive-class performance because FPR stays low even with many FPs. AUC-PR focuses directly on precision and recall — measuring positive-class identification quality. Prefer AUC-PR when positives are rare and important.
questions.source: Davis & Goadrich. Precision-Recall and ROC Curves. ICML, 2006; Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022.

questions.stem: Three models on a medical diagnosis task (disease prevalence=5%): Model A: Accuracy=97%, Recall=0.10. Model B: Accuracy=91%, Recall=0.82. Model C: Accuracy=95%, Recall=0.60. Which should be deployed and why?

options.A: Model A — highest accuracy means best performance
options.B: Model B — highest recall (0.82) correctly identifies 82% of disease cases. In medical diagnosis, missing disease (FN) is life-threatening; the 6pp accuracy cost is acceptable given the 72pp recall gain over Model A
options.C: Model C — best balance of accuracy and recall
options.D: None — all three have inadequate performance for medical use

questions.explanation: Model A's 97% accuracy exploits class imbalance (95% healthy baseline) — it misses 90% of sick patients. Model B sacrifices 6pp accuracy but identifies 82% of cases. In high-stakes medical diagnosis, maximising recall (minimising missed diagnoses) is the primary objective. Model C's 0.60 recall leaves 40% undetected — still unacceptable. FDA/CE Medical standards typically mandate sensitivity thresholds.
questions.source: Géron. Hands-On ML 3rd ed. 2022; James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.4.
